front: Union: personal union of Denmark, Norway, and Sweden lasting from 1397 to 1523
Union: named for the castle town of Kalmar on Sweden's Baltic coast, where it was proclaimed
Union: each of its three constituent kingdoms was governed by its own council, called a Riksradet
Union: dominated throughout by Denmark, whose capital Copenhagen served as its capital
Ruler: engineered the union after the deaths of her husband Haakon VI of Norway and her son Olaf II
Union: founded explicitly to counter the trading power of the Hanseatic League in the Baltic Sea
Ruler: mockingly nicknamed "King Pantsless" by the defeated Albert of Mecklenburg
Union: two foundation charters included the "Letter of Coronation" and a second document used to force concession of castles and len to a subregion's natives after a rebellion
Ruler: born Bogislav, changed his name upon becoming the union's first crowned king at a triple coronation at Kalmar in 1397
Ruler: his queen Philippa of Lancaster served as regent during his pilgrimage to Jerusalem
Ruler: their marriage was part of a failed plan to also wed his sister to the Prince of Wales, bringing a defensive alliance with England
Ruler: instituted the Sound Dues, a toll on ships passing between the Baltic and North Seas enforced by land-based cannon
Ruler: deposed after the Engelbrekt Rebellion, went into exile, and briefly took command of a pirate fleet
Ruler: failed in his attempt to impose his cousin Boguslav as his successor
Ruler: nicknamed the "Bark King" after a series of bad harvests forced peasants to eat tree bark
Event: peasants successfully used wagons as defensive barriers against Christopher of Bavaria's forces at this battle
Ruler: secured the Swedish throne with the help of regent Sten Sture the Elder, who later revolted against him at the Battle of Brunkeberg
work: Christian I and the Sture Regencies

front: Ruler: his visit to Italy is commemorated by Romanino's extensive frescoes at Malpaga Castle
work: Christian I and the Sture Regencies

front: Ruler: invaded Sweden in 1520, allying with Archbishop Gustavus Trolle against regent Sten Sture the Younger
work: Christian II and the Stockholm Bloodbath

front: Event: massacre orchestrated by Christian II in which Sten Sture the Younger's body was exhumed and burnt at the stake
work: Christian II and the Stockholm Bloodbath

front: Ruler: the Pope's refusal to remove his ally Archbishop Gustavus Trolle helped push Sweden toward Protestantism
work: Christian II and the Stockholm Bloodbath

front: Ruler: was overthrown, exiled to the Netherlands, and imprisoned for 27 years by his successor Frederick I
work: Christian II and the Stockholm Bloodbath

front: Ruler: escaped the Stockholm Bloodbath and led a liberation war, crowned king of an independent Sweden at the cathedral in Strangnas in 1523
Ruler: confiscated monastery lands during the Swedish Reformation and appointed Laurentius Petri the first Lutheran Archbishop of Uppsala
Event: Olaus Petri, brother of the first Lutheran Archbishop of Uppsala, published the first Swedish New Testament
Event: 1542 peasant revolt from Smaland against Gustav Vasa's introduction of Lutheranism, which defeated a force of German landsknechte mercenaries before being crushed
Event: this 1814 convention, following the Treaty of Kiel, forced a defeated Norway into a new personal union with Sweden that lasted until 1905
work: Legacy: Denmark-Norway, the Sound Dues, and Finland

front: Event: after the union's fall, the resulting Kingdom of Denmark-Norway's Privy Council unilaterally reduced Norway to the status of a province
work: Legacy: Denmark-Norway, the Sound Dues, and Finland

front: Event: the Sound Dues toll on Baltic shipping, instituted during the union, was not abolished until this year
work: Legacy: Denmark-Norway, the Sound Dues, and Finland

front: Union: Finland, then under Swedish rule, was also a part of it
work: Legacy: Denmark-Norway, the Sound Dues, and Finland
